data.title: Invesco Ltd. 2024 Q3 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Invesco Ltd. in conjunction with their 2024 Q3 earnings call.

data.title: Viasat: Best To Stay On The Sidelines
data.description:
Introduction

Viasat, Inc. (NASDAQ:VSAT), a small-cap proxy on the global communication technology and services market, has experienced a disastrous 12 months. Granted, it's not as if other small-cap technology stocks have dazzled either, but at least, they've managed to stay afloat, with almost a flattish return profile over a 1-year basis; conversely, VSAT has been taken to the cleaners, losing over 60% of its value.

With the stock now not far from dropping to single-digit levels (the last time the stock floundered at such low levels was over two decades ago, in 2003), is there some salvation to be found at these beaten-down levels, or could we see more of the same?

Still Plenty Of Concerns

VSAT offers a plethora of connectivity solutions, but it currently reports under two broad segments- Communication Services, and Defense and Advanced Technologies. Of the two, the latter takes greater precedence as it contributes 77% of total sales, and 83% of total EBITDA, and it doesn't help when your main contributor isn't growing.

For the uninitiated, VSAT offers broadband and narrowband services for markets such as government, aviation, residential, maritime, energy, etc. In Q1, note that revenue for this segment had dropped by 2% (on a combined basis), driven in large part by weakness seen in the fixed services and other segment (accounted for the largest share of communication services revenue last year at 30%) which is the proxy for fixed broadband services for residential and business clients.

This is not a one-off, and for the rest of the year, VSAT expects US fixed broadband revenue to remain challenged (mainly on account of weak residential subscription, driven in part by Starlink's entry), and play a part in ensuring that revenue for the communication services sees a decline for the whole year.

Within Communication Services, one of the other businesses that appears to be faring rather well (19% growth in Q1) is the aviation services business, which also contributes 30% of the segment revenue. But, this is a segment that is expected to see plenty of sequential volatility, and with the latest reports pointing to how commercial airline delivery delays have not improved, investors would do well not to get too euphoric. Worryingly, investors should also be concerned by the growing trend of airlines moving their in-flight Wi-Fi to Starlink's laser-linked satellites. VSAT has of late been winning a healthy chunk of IFS (In-flight service) contracts, but one does wonder if this will start ebbing. It's worth noting that VSAT's order backlog has already been on a declining trend for a while now.

Then, for a business with close to $7.5bn in gross debt, and around $400-$450m of interest costs p.a, it is quite troubling to note that VSAT does not generate positive EBIT on an FY basis.

Meanwhile, over the past decade, VSAT's operating cash flow has never been meaningful enough to cover either its CAPEX commitments or its debt burden. The net leverage in Q1 may have dipped to 3.5x, but management expects this to increase from current levels, and end higher by the end of FY25.

On the CAPEX front, we admit that the growth component should start easing as more of VSAT's high-throughput satellites go into operation (ViaSat-3F1 went live in August, and ViaSat-3F2 and ViaSat-3F3 are expected to go live in mid to late 2025), but even the FY26 guide calls for CAPEX commitments to the tune of $1.2bn, which certainly isn't a pittance.

Prima facie, at a forward EV/EBITDA of just 4.3x, VSAT may come across as very cheap, more so as it translates to a rough 40% discount over its long-term average of around 7x.

However, dig a little deeper, and you'll begin to wonder if you're getting good enough EBITDA growth for such a low multiple. Firstly, don't get carried away by the 16% EBITDA growth seen in Q1-25, as this pace will likely slow as we progress through the rest of the year; indeed, management has only guided to a mid-single-digit YoY increase in EBITDA growth for the whole of FY25, which would imply a much slower pace of growth for the rest of the year.

Looking beyond next year, and through FY27, it does not appear that VSAT offers compelling EBITDA growth either. In fact, taking last year's adjusted EBITDA of $1.488bn as a base, and looking at consensus numbers for the next three years, it appears we have a business that is poised only to generate a 3-year CAGR of 3% (for context, over the past three years, VSAT's historical 3-year CAGR has been a lot better at over 12%), which is not a great threshold of growth for a business priced at 4x EBITDA.

Investors may also want to consider that VSAT has a history of seeing significant EBITDA revisions; for context, note that next year's EBITDA numbers have already been trimmed by 7% over the last six months, so don't rule out further contractions, given the headwinds in some of their markets.

Closing Thoughts - What Do The Charts Suggest?

It's also rather clear that large swathes of the market, don't have a favorable perspective on VSAT's prospects now, and this is reflected in the charts. If you want to bet against the trend, good luck to you!

On the daily chart, bearish conditions can be reiterated by the price trading well below its key moving averages - the 50-day MA, 100-day MA, and 200-day MA.

This will embolden the bears, who have once again started boosting their short positions. Note that the percentage of float that is short had been on a slightly declining trend through August, but has once again started trending up since September and currently stands at 16%.

Meanwhile, one may also be discouraged to note that the smart money also appears to be bailing from this counter after they had shown some encouraging signs of buying the dip in June and July. For context, the net shares owned by institutions are now down by over 14% over the past three months!

To get a bird's eye view of VSAT's price action, one may also look at VSAT's monthly chart, and here we can see that bearish momentum is still in full flow, with a series of lower-lows (LLs), and lower-highs (LHs). We've also seen the price break down from strong support in 2003 and 2008, and despite some tenuous attempts to recoup that zone, we didn't get sufficient positive momentum.

Until we see some abatement in the series of LLs and LHs and a flattening out of the price action, we don't think investors should take the plunge, as the RSI is yet to hit the oversold zone, and the price is also not trading below its lower Bollinger band (which basically implies that the selling is far from overdone).

data.title: SOXL: Playing With Fire
data.description:
It's been nearly impossible to lose money in the semiconductor space over the last couple of years. Since the end of 2021, the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) has produced a massive 51% total return against a broad market that has been closer to 34% — which is still arguably terrific, just not as terrific as semis.

There can be little doubt the entire semiconductor space has benefited from the AI-driven excitement made possible by companies like Nvidia (NVDA). To the chagrin of some, myself included, NVDA continues to prove doubters wrong and make new highs. However, things have started to favor broad market equities over the semiconductor ETF since July. The SOXX chart doesn't look all that great in my view:

For those who practice technical analysis, it appears as though we have a head and shoulders pattern forming on the SOXX chart. This is typically viewed as bearish. In my view, this trend in SOXX is something that everyone playing in the Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bull 3X Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:SOXL) should be paying attention to.

Fund Details and Performance

I've covered leveraged ETFs for Seeking Alpha a handful of times. My general take has typically been that leveraged funds are terrible long-term investment products that should be avoided entirely by most traders and investors. SOXL is a fund that tracks many of the same positions that can be observed in SOXX. But it does so with 3x leverage and a daily re-balancing strategy that should ultimately lead to under-performance of the fund. This hasn't stopped SOXL shares from amassing a sizable $11.2 billion AUM since launching over a decade ago:

Fund Details SOXL
Expense Ratio 0.76%
Yield 0.88%
Assets Under Management $11.22b
Inception 3/11/2010

Source: Seeking Alpha.

As with any leveraged ETF, it's important to view SOXL against an un-leveraged semiconductor comp over time, so investors can get a sense for what happens if they don't make entries and exits appropriately. SOXL ripped a nearly 400% gain in less than a year if we start at the end of October 2023:

However, since that time, SOXL has managed to keep just a 137% gain against a 56.3% gain in SOXX. So what's the problem? What if instead of buying the bottom, we bought the top of the semis in mid-July via SOXL?

In that instance, we're down over 43% against an un-leveraged semiconductor ETF that has corrected closer to 10-20%. At present, the 3x leveraged ETF has actually produced 4x the losses if we measure from the top of the July rally. Of course, it's perhaps less fair to judge these things from tops and bottoms since buyers and sellers will almost always fail to execute with such precision. But even if we observe through just the beginning of the year, we see the leveraged fund not holding up over time:

As shown, year to date, SOXX is up just under 20% while SOXL is up only 12.3%. Meaning, not only is SOXL not producing a 3x semiconductor return so far this year, but holding it over this length of time would produce a result that lags the broader market. I think the point is clear, SOXL can move dramatically higher when semis are crushing it with consistency. But buyers of the fund shares have to really get the timing right and can't be afraid to lock in wins when they have them. Thus, the fundamental setup for the companies that SOXL tracks are important. Is it time to be jumping into SOXL given the pullback in the semi space?

Top Ten Holdings & Insider Moves

The top ten holdings in SOXL account for 58% of the total fund capital. If it is profitability you seek, I don't know that we can ask much more from the top ten companies in the semi space. Almost all of these tickers have an A+ profitability grade from Seeking Alpha:

I've sorted those names by Seeking Alpha Quant Score in the picture above. Despite the terrific profitability grades, only two of the ten have a Quant Score over 4. Furthermore, six of the ten stocks have a valuation grade of D+ or worse. Of course, companies can stay overvalued (or overbought) for a very long time. Without a clear catalyst that triggers a revaluation, overvalued isn't necessarily a sell indication all on its own. Companies can certainly grow into valuations over time.

However, I think we can often get signals from what insiders are doing. And the Q3 insider sales story isn't all that encouraging:

Top Ten Holdings Q3 Insider Sales Q4 Insider Sales
Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) $27.3 -
Broadcom Limited (AVGO) $51.3 $22.0
Nvidia $930.7 $40.7
Qualcomm (QCOM) $6.7 $2.4
Texas Instruments (TXN) $17.7 -
Micron Technology (MU) $1.2 -
Intel (INTC) Net buyers -
Applied Materials (AMAT) - -
Lam Research (LRCX) $38.1 $0.5
Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR) $95.0 $12.5

Source: OpenInsider, values in millions.

Looking at each of these tickers individually, insider buying is nearly non-existent, while insider selling remains an ongoing story. Of the ten stocks listed above, only Intel had insider buying in the 3rd quarter. Applied Materials didn't have any insider sales in Q3, but that company did endure about $114 million of insider sales in June alone. For the third quarter, there was over $1.1 billion of insider sales in total for all of these companies.

Now, I'd be remiss if I didn't mention that the tickers above generally have multi-billion valuations. Thus, the insider selling may not be concerning to every investor. Furthermore, most of the $1.1 billion sale total is due to Jensen Huang selling what amounts to a small fraction of his NVDA holdings. But the larger point that I'm trying to convey here is leveraged ETFs do better when there is a clear-sustained trend higher. I'm not sure if we can make the case that SOXL has that given the technicals, valuations, and insider activity.

Final Takeaways

Leveraged ETFs are financial products that should exclusively be used by skilled traders with experience. Please review the risks of leveraged ETFs as explained in the linked SEC bulletin before placing any trades in this product. These risks include extreme volatility, time and volatility decay, and the real possibility of losing all of your investment. Only experienced traders who fully understand the risks should trade them. This article further explains the important risks of such ETFs with examples.

This is especially true for 3x leveraged ETFs like SOXL. Daily rebalancing of leveraged funds historically leads to long-term decay and underperformance against the underlying assets that these types of funds track. SOXL is no different. But as I view it, the risk in going long SOXL today is heightened for a few reasons; technically, the semis look bad. Fundamentally, they're overvalued. And while overvaluation isn't necessarily an immediate reason to sell, insiders of almost every single Top 10 SOXL stock certainly have been.

The time to buy SOXL was October 2023. 9 months later, the fund had returned roughly 400%. It's been roughly cut in half since, even while NVDA is still at an all-time high. Things can, and probably will, get much worse for SOXL traders who hold shares too long. I'd avoid it.

data.title: Stride, Inc. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Stride, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Santander: Still A Good Return, Still Say 'Buy' (But No Longer Cheap)
data.description:
It's time for me to provide my quarterly update for Banco Santander, S.A. (NYSE:SAN). Since I last wrote about the bank, we're only up around 3%, with the S&P 500 up around 5%. But I look at the return since late 2023 and see that there's still a fair bit of outperformance here for the company. Moreover, I remain firm in my stance which I've held previously that Santander is an undervalued company. You can find that particular article here.

In this article, we'll take an updated look at 2Q24, a preview for 2Q23, and see what this bank can deliver for you in the next 1-5 years.

I remain heavily invested in financials and banks. Santander may not be the largest of these investments, nor is it the highest yielding, but it nonetheless represents an attractive play for those looking for a good opportunity in banking.

It's the combination of what I view as stability here, with an A+ credit rating, which the company still has, and all the operating improvements Santander has delivered. Oh, and did I mention that the company is expecting 2024E earnings to keep growing above 15% and that this bank, unlike others across Europe does not expect EPS to decline in 2025-2026E?

Let's see what we have here, and why this may be a play for you and at what price I would start looking at a rotation here.

Santander: Upside in banking at less than 6.5x P/E normalized

In reviewing Santander now in October 2024, we have the 1H24 results and are close to reviewing 3Q24. As such, this will serve both as a look-back and a sort of earnings preview for the upcoming quarter.

To give you the short version of this half year for Santander, the results were amazing/very good. I use this wording with a justification based on the company improvements in profit, in efficiency, in ROTE, in CET-1, and in most other KPIs related to this bank's overall performance. Santander has been driving record results for a number of quarters at this point, but a 20% improvement year-over-year is still nothing to sneeze at - and 2Q24 saw an improvement of 20% in operating attributable profit up to €3.2B, meaning we have a run rate of over €12B in attributable profit for the company at this time. Even on a 1H24 basis, the annualized rate is over €12B with €6.1B of half-year profit, an improvement of 16%.

While Santander is still not the "king" of efficiency and C/I ratios, it has taken steps to improve. I would say a bank is doing well when they reach sub-38% C/I ratios, based on where European peers and Scandinavian leaders manage it. Santander is at 41.6% - this is still a drop of over 260 bps since last I covered the company here.

The two advantages here were customer revenue increases and much better efficiencies. The company saw, despite a normalizing interest rate, record NII, record fees, total revenue, and other KPIs.

In short, Santander is now on track to exceed its 2024E targets.

The targets and forecasts you see there are in turn backed by the ONE transformation, the company's improvement and savings program that I have covered in previous articles. In FY22, the company's C/I was 45.8%. Remember that the 2025E target was 42%. We have 41.6% now. Is this a fluke and likely to "bounce back" up? We'll see - but I do not believe this to be the case.

Santander is one of the largest financial institutions in the world. It has everything you might want from a bank, covering retail, consumer, CIB, wealth management, and payment systems. My personal experience with the bank's services is somewhat limited - my corporate car lease is "located" at Santander, and what contact I've had with their customer service has been absolutely hassle-free, if at times having to find contact info.

The company remains primarily a retail bank. 52% of the group's revenue contribution comes from the retail sector, which also has the second-best C/I and some of the best ROTE. The least efficient one of the company's operations is also its newest - payments, with a C/I that remains above 46.5% and is actually up 50 bps for the quarter. On the other hand, the payment segment also has the second-highest margins/returns in terms of all the company segments.

The global scale that the bank has, and the advantages that this brings, can clearly be seen in the company's operations - retail chief among them.

While the consumer business is tricky, the company has managed to grow loans by 6%, and deposits by more than twice that, while dropping the C/I to almost below 40%. The company's profit in the segment was, for the first time, above €1B alone, and this is despite a slight drop in total customers. What impresses me here, and why I raise the point, is the company managing a 7% revenue growth, and flat costs, with growth from both auto and non-auto (Santander remains a major auto financier).

Wealth for Santander has always been a bit interesting to me - and it's obvious that it's also interesting to its consumers, with an AUM growth of 20%, collab fees of 12% higher, and good revenue growth. PB is up significantly, meaning more than single digits.

Payment is a segment to definitely keep an eye on given both the cost and return potential. Revenue is up, transactions are up, and costs on a per-transaction basis are finally going down.

The company has delivered capex optimizations, operational efficiencies, more transactions, and good progress in the global cards platform, called Plard, with a forward focus on improving scale.

Shareholders have not gone poor from this improvement either. TNAVps and cash DPS (payouts) are up, with buybacks (though these, in my view, are becoming less appealing here as the valuation grows). It bears mentioning, however, that Santander has now bought back about 11% of shares outstanding, which also goes some way to explain the performance we've seen since the late part of 2023.

Risks and concerns?

As before, some of the concerns I have are some of the company's South American geographies, namely Argentina. While South America is one of the company's major drivers, and both LATAM and SA do need to be part of Santander, it's undeniable that these geographies, not only with FX but with political instability, cause some of the issues we're seeing here.

The company has tried to offset this by using an inflation-adjusted FX for Argentina and the volatile currency there, which has improved things, but this risk still remains.

Aside from this, I do not see as many risks anymore. Automotive exposure is one of them, sure - but Santander has worked diligently to reduce it - and the widening NII spreads and NIM expansion is showing some upsides here as well.

Of course, Santander still does have one of the highest CoR rates of any bank I invest in. The company's geographical exposure does mean that credit losses are more common here, and the best that can be said is that Stage 3 NPLs are down to below €36B. The coverage ratio for the bank is 66%, with an NPL of 3.02% and a CoR of 1.21%. That's one of the least impressive in any financial investment I've made and is also part of the reason for the company's low valuation.

But in context, this is an undeniable improvement, with year-to-date CoR down despite increasing mortgages, significant improvement in NPLs of 8 bps and otherwise seeing normalization.

Valuation for Santander: A target upgrade in terms of PT, and still a 17% upside to a five-year normalized P/E.

Santander is, as I see it, still a bank worth a "Buy." The company is no longer cheap, I cannot say that - but I believe the long-term upsides and positives I've considered likely for Santander have now materialized. Moreover, I believe they're likely to continue materializing and continue to see upside in this company. This is despite what I would consider a below-average sort of yield for a financial company - seeing as it's less than 3.5%.

But in this case, the upside is made up as much of reversal and growth as of dividends because Santander is expected to continue to grow its earnings more than other banks in the same sector or geography (Source: FAST Graphs paywalled link).

This means, based on current estimates, that the company is expected to generate annualized returns in excess of 15%, my personal "minimum" target thanks to an average EPS growth of 6.22%, a dividend of 3.04%, and operational leverage and upside. The rate of return at the five-year average of 7.18x P/E comes to over 17.5% per year, which is a good return. On the risk side, we can clarify that Santander historically has more often negatively missed its estimates than hit them - and this is something to discount for. But it's my conviction that this reflects a more backward-looking perspective for Santander, at a time when trends and the company's earnings profile and management were different.

I do not believe that this Santander will be a company that sees such a miss in earnings in the next few years - not between NIM/NII upside, and a slow recovery of interest rate upsides, given its exposures to LATAM and South America.

In fact, we can even argue that the company could be valued against its 20-year average. That 20-year average is 11.14x, which implies a price point of $10/share and an upside in the triple digits, or 41.7% annualized.

However, I do not consider such a development likely - which is why I stick to more conservative targets.

I do, however, raise my PT for Santander to that 7.5x, reflecting the upside I see here, and when I would consider a different avenue for my holding of shares. That PT comes to $5.25, and this is my new target.

For 3Q24, I do not expect anything except a solid quarter. I don't expect a further outperformance or raising of guidance - but that's not needed after 1H24. I believe the company will already outperform its original 2024 target.

My thesis updated for 2024A 2Q is as follows.

Thesis

  • Santander is one of the better banks found in the Southern European/LATAM/SA area and geography. It has significant emerging market exposure, which gives it a heightened risk profile in context to other banks - but it's also immensely qualitative, as evidenced by how it has survived previous downturns.
  • I consider Santander a "Buy" at cheap valuations, and I believe this bank can deliver significant upside over time - and now is one of those times.
  • I believe the company's thesis and potential have improved since my last article, and I continue to add shares, albeit at a lower rate than some other banks.
  • I upgrade my share price target for Santander to a PT of $5.25/share, a share price upgrade, and give it a "Buy," though the upside here is almost gone.
  • This thesis has been updated for 2024, and I remain positive here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative and well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills three out of three quality indicators and one out of two valuation indicators. Based on this, and a valuation target of $5.25/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Buy" rating as of October 2024.

data.title: Akamai: Delivery Headwinds And Compute Uncertainty
data.description:
I previously suggested that there were warning signs heading into Akamai's (NASDAQ:AKAM) first quarter earnings, which proved to be the case, as Akamai's share price declined more than 10% post-earnings. The stock has subsequently bounced back, with investors warming up to the growth potential of the Security and Compute businesses. The outcome of Akamai's investments in these areas are unclear though. In particular, the pursuit of cloud computing is a risky strategic move due to the upfront costs involved and comes in an uncertain macro environment where many companies are gambling on infrastructure buildouts.

Market Conditions

The CDN market has been soft in recent quarters due to macroeconomic and geopolitical headwinds. While this is weighing on Akamai’s business at the moment, the company doesn’t expect this situation to persist long term. The company has pulled back on investments in this area but wants to remain the CDN market leader, both because it is a profitable business and because there are synergies with Compute and Security.

Fastly (FSLY) has faced challenges within its largest customers recently, which appears to have been due to a combination of pricing pressure, weak traffic growth and the distribution of traffic across multiple vendors. Fastly reportedly hasn’t lost any accounts though. The softness in traffic is primarily coming from large media accounts, with these organizations engaging in a renewed push for profitability.

While there have been suggestions of pricing pressure and customers shifting traffic toward low-cost providers, Akamai has denied this. Akamai has suggested that pricing pressure is in line with historical norms, although the impact has been elevated by a lack of traffic growth. In terms of traffic, gaming and video have been unusually weak.

Akamai Business Updates

Delivery currently only accounts for around one third of Akamai’s revenue, down from roughly two thirds five years ago. In addition to current market headwinds, Akamai is being selective in regard to which traffic it serves, in an attempt to maintain profitability. The Delivery customer base remains important though, if for no other reason than the cross-selling opportunity it provides.

Security is now the largest part of Akamai's business and is currently supporting the company's topline as Delivery revenue declines. The security portfolio now includes DDoS prevention, Bot management, account and content protection, app and API security, and zero trust enterprise security.

Akamai announced its Guardicore platform in the second quarter, which combines micro segmentation, zero trust network access, multi-factor authentication, DNS firewall and threat hunting. Guardicore utilizes a single agent and provides customers with a unified control console.

Microsegmentation helps to stop lateral movement and secure critical IT assets using software-based segmentation with granular control. It segments a network into small sections that have their own security policies and are accessed separately. Next-generation firewalls are used to create segments, which provides application awareness.

While Akamai believes that Guardicore provides differentiation, microsegmentation competition is increasing. Illumio is probably the leading competitor, although Akamai believes that Guardicore is a better solution. Illumio had over $100 million bookings in the fourth quarter of 2023 and counts around 20% of the Fortune 100 as customers.

Akamai acquired Noname Security in the second quarter for $450 million, strengthening its API security solution. Akamai's guidance suggests that the acquisition price was at a revenue multiple of well over 10. Noname Security is still currently small though and should benefit significantly from Akamai's sales capacity. API Security is also a sizeable market (~$1 billion opportunity by 2027) that is expanding rapidly. F5 Networks (FFIV) is quite bullish on this market at the moment due to the potential impact of AI. The company's API Security customer count more than tripled YoY, even though AI tailwinds are yet to really kick in.

Akamai's Compute business also continues to expand, although most of its revenue is coming from existing customers. Large media customers are unsurprisingly the biggest segment for the compute business. New customer additions are reportedly growing rapidly though. The compute opportunity is orders of magnitude bigger than the delivery opportunity and as a result, Akamai expects its Compute business to be much bigger than the delivery business. For example, Akamai has suggested that large media companies spend up to 10x more on compute than delivery.

Akamai has offered Function-as-a-Service on its edge platform for many years and can now offer full-stack cloud computing, enabling customers to run virtual machines and containers on its platform. The cloud solution is focused on performance, scalability and costs, with the company believing that there are synergies with Security and Delivery. So far, popular customer use cases include live transcoding, secure access, observability, object storage and real-time log aggregation. AI workloads reportedly aren’t a meaningful contributor at the moment, but Akamai believes this could be a growth driver longer term.

Financial Analysis

Akamai generated $980 million in revenue in the second quarter, an increase of 5% YoY. Weakness in the Delivery business was offset by solid Compute and Security growth. Akamai suggested that Guardicore Zero Trust and API Security were bright spots. While acquisitions have been a growth driver, Noname contributed less than $1 million in revenue in the second quarter.

While Akamai's growth rate is currently poor, absolute growth from its Security and Compute businesses is in line with Cloudflare’s (NET) absolute revenue growth. This could be considered a positive signal, but it is worth bearing in mind that growth is being driven by the adoption of Security and Compute solutions by Akamai's Delivery customers. When this opportunity is exhausted, Akamai may find it difficult to continue generating decent growth.

Noname is expected to contribute $8-$10 million revenue in the third quarter and around $10 million in Q4. The Paris Olympics are also expected to provide a $3-$4 million boost. As a result, Akamai is projecting $988-$1,008 million revenue for the third quarter, representing a 2-4% YoY growth rate.

The number of job openings mentioning Akamai in the job requirements highlights the ongoing weak demand environment. The number of Akamai job openings is also suggestive of weak growth going forward.

Akamai’s gross margins continue to slide, despite new products having high gross margins. Modest revenue growth and declining gross margins are making it difficult for Akamai to maintain its operating profit margin. This situation is being compounded by the fact that Akamai is now being forced to invest in R&D to maintain growth.

It is also somewhat surprising given that third-party cloud spend is expected to decline by over two thirds as a result of Akamai migrating most of its own apps to Akamai Connected Cloud, saving the company well over $100 million annually.

The company is maintaining a 30% near-term operating margin goal, with potential upside from the faster growing parts of its business. I don't really see where gains will come from though, given Delivery headwinds and the fact that Akamai's newer businesses require greater investments.

CapEx is currently sitting at around 13% of revenue and rising, as a result of Akamai's expanding cloud business, which is more capital intensive. CapEx for the delivery business is in the low single digit range as a percentage of revenue, which Akamai expects to be maintained as a result of avoiding traffic with large peaks.

Conclusion

While Akamai's share price has moved significantly higher in recent months, its valuation still appears reasonable, particularly if the company can return its margins to historical norms. Cloudflare's valuation is illustrative of the extent to which Akamai's share price could move higher if the company's strategy pays off, the business accelerates, and sentiment improves. Conversely, Fastly's valuation indicates the downside risk from low growth and margins.

Akamai's revenue growth rate is only expected to be in the low single digits going forward though, meaning that the company's share price is being supported by its profitability, and in particular, ability to generate free cash flow.

This situation makes Akamai's investments in cloud computing critical. Akamai is not alone in thinking that there is an opportunity here, as F5 Networks is engaged in a similar effort and there are rumors that Cloudflare is considering acquiring DigitalOcean (DOCN). While there may be some advantages to offering an integrated cloud and edge network, I question whether Akamai's service will have broad appeal, particularly given the company's legacy edge network architecture. I also question whether Akamai will have the economies of scale to be competitive in this area long-term. Between CapEx and acquisitions, the company has invested over $3 billion in the last three years. In that time, the company's revenue is only up by around $600 million though. Investors will probably want to see greater returns on these investments if Akamai's stock price is to continue moving higher.

data.title: Encouraging Signs For Mortgage REITs
data.description:
Mortgage REITs or mREITs have generally been weak investments with a history of book value erosion. Most of the erosion has been caused by shocks to the financial system or unfavorable macro conditions. As such, we generally stay away from common shares of mREITs finding the preferreds more opportunistic.

The macro environment is shifting, however, in ways that make the future of mREITs look significantly brighter. We believe the majority of mREITs will have strong 3Q24 reports as well as improved forward earnings outlooks. This could provide an opportunistic trading window for common shares but also bolsters the reliability of preferred dividend payments.

Favorable Changes

There are 4 significant positive changes to the macro environment in which mREITs operate:

  • Reduced RMBS spreads improve book value.
  • Alleviated negative convexity.
  • Lower cost of capital.
  • Positive carry returns with an uninverted yield curve.

Let us discuss each in more detail.

Reduced RMBS Spreads

Residential Mortgage Backed Security or RMBS yields are correlated with Treasury yields, but the premium over Treasuries at which they trade is variable. Mortgage spreads are the difference between RMBS yields and Treasury yields.

Spreads are typically closer to 110 basis points, but they gapped up to about 200 basis points in the chaos following the onset of COVID.

In recent quarters, however, RMBS spreads have started to drop back down, now sitting at 132 basis points.

Lower spreads have 2 main effects:

  1. Increased market value of existing instruments.
  2. Lower going-in ROIC.

New capital has not really benefitted from lower spreads because the yield is now lower, but mREITs already own billions of dollars of RMBS. These financial instruments that were already on their books had to trade up in price to match the now lower Yield to Maturity.

Thus, due to reduced RMBS spreads, most mREITs that deal in RMBS should see significant book value gains in 3Q24. Dynex Capital, Inc. (DX) and AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC) were the first to report in the sector and both had nice book value gains. We anticipate others will follow suit.

Alleviated Negative Convexity

With fixed duration bonds the relationship between price and yield is quite simple.

  • Yields up = prices down.
  • Yields down = prices up.

RMBS is different because its duration can change with yields. Lower yields encourage prepayment which can reduce expected duration. Thus, the pricing impact of a change in yield is unclear because the shift in expected duration can offset the price movement needed to cure the yield to maturity.

Since prepayment is entirely at the option of the borrower, it is strictly a bad thing for any RMBS trading at a premium to par. RMBS trading at a premium to par has the following reactions to changes in yield:

  • Yields up = prices down.
  • Yields down = prices up or down depending on whether the negative convexity impact on expected duration outweighs the normal price reaction.

If premiums are large enough, which was the case in the zero interest rate environment where mortgage yields dropped to around 2%, RMBS was set up to have a negative price reaction whether yields went up or down.

It was a true "damned if you do, damned if you don't" situation.

They had to either face higher yields which dropped prices or higher prepayment rates which also dropped prices because expected duration decreased.

Today's outlook is much better because negative convexity is not a problem when RMBS is trading at a discount.

Most of the mortgages currently outstanding were initially signed at lower coupons than current mortgage rates. Therefore, a large portion of RMBS is trading at a discount to par.

With discounted RMBS, prepayment is favorable. If you buy a mortgage at 97 cents on the dollar, getting back full par value ahead of schedule is a good thing. Therefore, RMBS-owning mREITs now benefit from falling interest rates.

Lower Cost of Capital

Repo rates tend to correlate with Fed Fund rates but often anticipate moves ahead of the Fed. As the Fed was hiking, repo rates went up in advance of Fed hikes and now repo rates are falling in anticipation of further cuts. For example, repo rates dropped in August, well before the Fed actually cut in September. Repo rates are anticipating further cuts as current repo rates sit below the current Fed Funds target rate.

Despite recent drops, repo rates remain well above normal levels which is more visible if we zoom out to a 5-year chart.

The cost of capital has declined a little bit for mREITs and is expected to decline more as Fed Funds reaches its terminal level. Of course, we don't know exactly where the Fed will end, but most analysts think somewhere in the vicinity of 350 basis points which implies mREIT cost of capital will continue to decline.

With a lower cost of capital, spreads on investment are improving.

Positive Carry from Yield Curve

The inverted yield curve has been challenging for mREITs in recent years. A portion of their income can come from borrowing short and owning higher duration assets such as RMBS. That portion of Net Interest Margin (NIM) does not exist when the curve is inverted and may be partially why book values have eroded precipitously in recent years.

The forward outlook looks better as the yield curve has been uninverted.

10 and 30-year Treasuries are already well above 2-year Treasuries, and it is anticipated that the short end will drop below the long end soon with just a couple more Fed cuts.

Dynex sees opportunity in uninversion as discussed by their Co-CEO on the 3Q24 earnings call; Smriti Laxman Popenoe - Co-CEO:

"From a macro perspective, we are moving into a regime with a less restricted Fed, which brings with it the opportunity to earn positive carry from the yield curve. This is a powerful source of forward returns."

In a sector that deals with small spreads and amplifies those spreads with leverage, every small factor that contributes to a wider NIM is quite impactful.

A better forward outlook for mREITs

We still don't think mREITs are a great asset class in general, but given the macro changes, they are looking significantly better. As such our change in investment allocation is that the hurdle is a bit lower.

In the formerly bad environment, it took an extreme discount for us to actually invest in mREIT common shares. Given the better environment, we would now consider investing in moderate discounts.

In other words, if 60% of book value was the threshold at which mREIT common shares were worth considering, maybe now it is 80% of book value.

Larger and better-managed mREITs like AGNC could potentially become opportunistic at 85%-90% of the book. Currently trading at a slight premium to book we aren't pulling the trigger, but it's close enough to go on the watchlist.

Potential ways to trade it

Given the tightening in RMBS spreads that happened in 3Q24 we see it is highly likely most mREITs will have strong 3Q earnings reports. There could be potential to capture pops on the release of strong earnings.

mREIT preferreds remain good investments where discounts to par are met with strong underlying company fundamentals. Dividend payments and liquidation preference of preferreds are freshly bolstered by the now higher book values of the companies and capital markets opening back up to common share issuance.

We continue to like Arbor Realty Trust, Inc. (ABR) preferreds, Chimera Investment Corporation (CIM) preferreds, and a few others that can be purchased opportunistically from time to time due to volatile short-term market pricing.

data.title: Bunge Global: Awaiting Q3 Earnings As The Shares Move Lower
data.description:
I wrote about Bunge Global SA (NYSE:BG) on Seeking Alpha in mid-May 2024 when the shares were at the $104.83 level. I highlighted the $80 to $120 range in BG shares, concluding:

I am a scale-down buyer of Bunge Global SA during periods of price weakness, leaving room to add on further declines. BG is not only a leading agricultural producer and processor, but also a potential takeover or merger candidate that pays an attractive dividend that reduces risk while waiting for capital appreciation.

On October 22, 2024, BG shares had moved 14.8% lower to the $89.33 level. While a scale-down approach has yielded losses, BG shares could be near a bottom if the ten-year range holds. The company will report earnings at the end of this month.

BG shares decline since the July 2024 high

BG shares have made lower highs and lower lows since the July 26, $114.92 peak.

The six-month chart highlights the decline in BG shares over the past months, with the stock near the bottom end of the decade-long trading range.

Factor and Dividend Grades have mostly deteriorated

BG’s Seeking Alpha Factor Grades moved mostly lower since mid-May 2024.

While BG’s valuation grade remains at an A since the mid-May 2024 article, growth fell from a D to a failing grade, profitability moved from B+ to B, and momentum dropped from a C+ to a D grade. The Revisions grade experienced a slight increase from a C+ to a B-.

Dividend grades fared slightly better, as the current $2.72 per share payout translates to a 3.04% yield.

Dividend safety fell from A- to B+, while dividend growth edged lower from B to B-. Consistency was consistent with a B rating, while the yield grade moved higher from a B+ to an A-.

The price action in the shares and consistency of the dividend caused the changes in Seeking Alpha’s factor and dividend grades.

Grain and oilseed prices have moved lower - Ethanol prices have declined

BG processes grains and oilseeds into food and fuel, making the company’s profits sensitive to the underlying commodity prices. Since May 16:

  • Nearby CBOT soybean futures prices declined 18.6% from $12.19 to $9.9175 per bushel.
  • Nearby CBOT corn futures prices declined 8.86% from $4.5700 to $4.1650 per bushel.
  • Nearby CBOT wheat futures prices declined 12.76% from $6.6025 to $5.7600 per bushel.
  • Nearby Chicago ethanol swap prices fell 13.00% from $1.8050 to $1.5700 per gallon wholesale.

While the leading grain and biofuel prices declined, sugar and soybean crush spreads moved higher since mid-May. Nearby ICE sugar futures rose 16.4% from 18.67 cents on May 16 to 21.73 cents per pound on October 22. The December soybean crush spread moved 73.4% higher from $1.0825 on May 16 to $1.8775 on October 22. While the crush and sugar have increased, grain, oilseed, and ethanol prices have declined, weighing on BG earnings margins.

BG earnings at the end of October

BG’s Q2 earnings were below the market’s consensus estimates:

With EPS and revenues below forecasted levels, the shares experienced pressure. BG will report Q3 earnings next week, with the current estimates above Q2 levels and revenues slightly lower:

The chart shows that the market expects higher EPS, but revenues are slightly lower than the Q2 $13.24 billion level at $13.20 billion.

The trend since 2020 remains bullish

As BG awaits Q3 earnings, the short-term trend remains bearish, with the stock moving towards the $80 per share support level. However, the longer-term chart has remained bullish since the 2020 low.

The five-year chart highlights the bullish price action that took BG shares 342.8% higher from the $29.00 March 2020 pandemic-inspired low to the $128.40 April 2022 high. CBOT wheat prices reached a record high in 2022, with corn and soybean futures rallying within pennies of their all-time peaks. Since then, the grain and oilseed futures markets have plunged, weighing on BG and other agricultural processors’ earnings.

Given BG’s significant exposure to Brazil, the price action in the U.S. dollar versus the Brazilian real exchange rate (USD:BRL) has also weighed on the shares.

The ten-year chart of the Brazilian real versus the U.S. dollar exchange rate shows the Brazilian currency is trading near the lows, weighing on earnings with Brazilian exposure.

As BG shares approach the $80 support level, I continue to favor a scale-down buying approach as the stock heads for the bottom end of its trading range. A positive earnings surprise on October 30 could ignite an overdue rally in BG, while further disappointment would likely cause the shares to challenge the $80 technical support. With BG near the bottom end of its range, I believe the company that processes the agricultural products that feed and increasingly power the world will hold the $80 level. Aside from earnings, the future path of least resistance of BG shares could depend on agricultural commodity prices and the Brazilian real’s strength or weakness against the U.S. dollar. BG is a proxy for agricultural commodities and Brazilian real exposure.

data.title: Northwest Bancshares: Over 6% Yield, But Downgrading To Hold Following Recent Rally
data.description:
Earnings of Northwest Bancshares, Inc. (NASDAQ:NWBI) will likely rebound after the second quarter's security restructuring. Loan growth will likely also return to last year's level after a disappointing quarter, leading to earnings growth in upcoming years. Overall, I'm expecting earnings to dip by 32% to $0.72 per share in 2024 before growing by 31% in 2025 to $0.94 per share. Compared to my last report on the company, I've reduced the earnings estimate for this year because of disappointing results for the second quarter of this year. The December 2025 target price is close to the current market price. Further, Northwest is offering an attractive dividend yield of 6.1%. Based on the total expected return, I'm downgrading Northwest Bancshares to a hold rating.

Restructuring to Lift, While Rate Cuts to Pressure the Margin

The net interest margin grew by 10 basis points during the second quarter of 2024 after four consecutive quarters of decline. The margin improvement was due to two factors: securities restructuring and deceleration of deposit migration.

During the second quarter, Northwest Bancshares sold off a large chunk of its securities portfolio at a loss of $39.4 million (pre-tax), which resulted in proceeds of $276 million, as mentioned in the earnings presentation. These outgoing securities had an average book yield of 1.79%. The management successfully reinvested most of the proceeds during the second quarter at a much higher yield of 6.0%. Although the restructuring was mostly completed in the second quarter, there was still some benefit left for the third quarter. The full-quarter impact of the restructuring will be seen in the third quarter.

The second factor for the margin's turnaround was the deposit migration slowdown. Previously, deposit migration had been a major reason for the margin's decline. A positive development during the second quarter was that the deposit mix deterioration slowed down. Management mentioned in the earnings presentation that the pace of volumes into higher-cost CDs and high-yield savings products began to slow, as shown in the table below.

I'm expecting deposit migration to become negligible soon because interest rate cuts will diminish the incentive of moving funds around and chasing yields. Therefore, the deposit migration factor will no longer affect the margin in the foreseeable future.

An important factor affecting the margin in the next year and a half is the interest rate down cycle. I'm expecting the Fed to cut the Fed funds rate by a further 25 basis points in the fourth quarter of 2024 and 75-100 basis points in 2025. This means that I'm expecting a total rate cut of 150-175 basis points from the third quarter of 2024 until the last quarter of 2025. The results of management's rate-sensitivity simulation model given in the 10-Q filing show that a 200-basis point rate cut could reduce the net interest income by 4.4% over twelve months.

Considering the recent restructuring and the ongoing rate down cycle, I'm expecting the net interest margin to have grown by three basis points in the third quarter of 2024. From the fourth quarter of 2024 until the end of 2025, I'm expecting the margin to dip by a single basis point each quarter.

Loan Growth to Improve as Payoffs Normalize

Northwest Bancshares' loan portfolio declined by 1.4% during the second quarter of the year, which was much worse than my expectations given in my last report on the company. Management mentioned on the conference call that a higher-than-expected level of runoffs in the portfolio was responsible for the decline. Management also mentioned that it was expecting runoffs to be less impactful going forward. Further, management expects loan growth to improve in the second half of this year as it mentioned on the conference call that it anticipates low-single-digit loan growth.

I, too, am optimistic about a recovery because the operating environment continues to be healthy. Northwest Bancshares' operations are concentrated in Pennsylvania. The company also has operations in Ohio, Indiana, and New York. As can be seen below, Pennsylvania's unemployment rate is currently doing better than not only the national average but also the state's own past.

Overall, I'm estimating the loan portfolio to have grown by 1.1% in the third quarter of 2024. Further, I'm expecting the loan portfolio to grow by 1.1% every quarter through the end of 2025. Compared to my last report on the company, I have reduced my loan balance estimate for 2024 because of the second quarter's disappointing performance. I haven't changed my growth estimate for 2025.

Moreover, I'm expecting deposits to grow in line with loans. The following table shows my balance sheet estimates.

Financial Position FY19 FY20 FY21 FY22 FY23 FY24E FY25E
Net Loans 8,751 10,446 9,914 10,802 11,290 11,468 11,981
Growth of Net Loans 9.4% 19.4% (5.1)% 9.0% 4.5% 1.6% 4.5%
Other Earning Assets 838 1,578 2,317 2,099 1,858 1,859 1,935
Deposits 8,592 11,599 12,301 11,465 11,980 12,355 12,907
Borrowings and Sub-Debt 368 457 392 924 643 496 516
Common equity 1,353 1,539 1,584 1,491 1,551 1,563 1,580
Book Value Per Share ($) 12.8 12.8 12.5 11.8 12.2 12.3 12.4
Tangible BVPS ($) 9.3 9.5 9.4 8.7 9.2 9.3 9.4

Source: SEC Filings, Analyst's Estimates.

(In USD million unless otherwise specified).

Earnings to Rebound

Northwest Bancshares's earnings tanked in the second quarter due to large losses on the sale of securities. I'm expecting earnings to have rebounded in the third quarter to near the first quarter's level as the security restructuring is over. While subdued loan growth will drive earnings, slight margin pressure will likely restrict earnings growth from the fourth quarter of 2024 until the end of 2025.

Additionally, I'm making the following assumptions.

  1. I'm expecting the provision expense for expected loan losses to return to the 2023 level after remaining subdued in the first half of 2024. This is because I'm expecting loan growth to also return to the 2023 level. Further, I'm expecting the economy to remain stable.
  2. Excluding the losses on the sale of securities, I'm expecting non-interest income to remain rangebound. As mentioned above, I'm not expecting the company to realize any more losses through sales of securities.
  3. I'm expecting the efficiency ratio to return to the 1Q level after it was elevated in the second quarter due to the security restructuring.

Overall, I'm expecting the company to report earnings of $0.72 per share for 2024, down 32%, and $0.94 per share for 2025, up 31%. In my last report on the company, which was issued in July, I forecast earnings of $0.92 per share for 2024 and $0.94 per share for 2025. I've reduced my estimate for this year because the security restructuring took me by surprise. Also, I hadn't expected the loan portfolio to decline in size during the second quarter. I haven't changed my estimate for 2025 much as my outlook remains more or less the same.

Northwest Bancshares is scheduled to announce its third-quarter results on October 29, 2024. For the reasons mentioned above, I'm estimating the company to report earnings of $0.22 per share for the quarter.

The following table shows my income statement estimates.

Income Statement FY19 FY20 FY21 FY22 FY23 FY24E FY25E
Net interest income 360 392 391 421 436 425 441
Provision for loan losses 23 84 (12) 28 23 15 24
Non-interest income 99 132 143 111 114 75 114
Non-interest expense 296 347 345 330 352 369 378
Net income - Common Sh. 110 75 153 134 135 91 119
EPS - Diluted ($) 1.04 0.62 1.21 1.05 1.06 0.72 0.94

Source: SEC Filings, Earnings Releases, Analyst's Estimates.

(In USD million unless otherwise specified).

Vehicle Loans are the Main Source of Risk

The biggest source of risk for Northwest Bancshares is its exposure to vehicle loans, which make up around 17% of total loans. Vehicle loans are by nature quite risky because the collateral (car, motorbike, etc.) can get damaged, and the amount may not be entirely recoverable from insurance in all cases.

Following the securities restructuring, Northwest Bancshares has reduced the riskiness associated with the securities portfolio. Unrealized losses on the Available-for-Sale securities portfolio were down to $173.6 million at the end of June from $196.9 million at the end of December 2023. To put the latest amount in perspective, $173.6 million is 11% of the equity book value. I'm expecting these remaining unrealized losses to reverse as interest rates decline. I'm not expecting the company to sell any more securities, thereby turning unrealized losses into realized losses.

Dividend Yield of 6.1%

Northwest Bancshares is offering an attractive dividend yield of 6.1%, assuming the company maintains its quarterly dividend at the current level of $0.20 per share. The earnings and dividend estimates suggest a payout ratio of 85% for 2025, which is quite high compared to other banks. Nevertheless, such a high payout ratio is not unusual for Northwest as it has averaged a payout ratio of 82% over the last five years. Therefore, I think the chances of a dividend cut are negligible.

Furthermore, Northwest Bancshares has excess capital, so it does not need to cut back on dividends to build up capital. The company reported a total capital-to-risk-weighted assets ratio of 16.674% for the end of June, which was much higher than the regulatory requirement of 10.50%.

Downgrading to Hold

When I last wrote a report on Northwest Bancshares in July, the last closing price was $11.51. I determined a December 2024 target price of $12.20 in that report. As my target price and dividend yield suggested a good expected return, I adopted a buy rating. Since then, the stock price has rallied by an impressive 11%, to reach a level above my previous target price.

As we're now close to the end of this year, I've decided to roll over my target price to the end of next year. I'm using the historical price-to-tangible book ("P/TB") and price-to-earnings ("P/E") multiples to value the company. Northwest Bancshares has traded at an average P/TB ratio of 1.43x and an average P/E ratio of around 13.71x in the past, as shown below.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple with the forecast tangible book value per share of $9.40 gives a target price of $13.40 for the end of 2025. This price target implies a 1.8% upside from the October 21 closing price. The following table shows the sensitivity of the target price to the P/TB ratio.

P/TB Multiple 1.23x 1.33x 1.43x 1.53x 1.63x
TBVPS - Dec 2025 ($) 9.4 9.4 9.4 9.4 9.4
Target Price ($) 11.6 12.5 13.4 14.4 15.3
Market Price ($) 13.2 13.2 13.2 13.2 13.2
Upside/(Downside) (12.4)% (5.3)% 1.8% 8.9% 16.0%

Source: Analyst's Estimates.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple with the forecast earnings per share of $0.94 gives a target price of $12.80 for the end of 2025. This price target implies a 2.8% downside from the October 21 closing price. The following table shows the sensitivity of the target price to the P/E ratio.

P/E Multiple 11.7x 12.7x 13.7x 14.7x 15.7x
EPS 2025 ($) 0.94 0.94 0.94 0.94 0.94
Target Price ($) 11.0 11.9 12.8 13.8 14.7
Market Price ($) 13.2 13.2 13.2 13.2 13.2
Upside/(Downside) (17.0)% (9.9)% (2.8)% 4.3% 11.4%

Source: Analyst's Estimates.

Equally weighting the target prices from the two valuation methods gives a combined target price of $13.10, which implies a 0.5% downside from the current market price. Adding the forward dividend yield gives a total expected return of 5.6%.

Based on the updated total expected return, I think it's time to downgrade Northwest Bancshares to a hold rating. The earnings outlook is good, but it has already gotten priced in since the issuance of my last report.

data.title: Viatris Ahead Of Q3 Earnings: No Longer A 'Screaming' Bargain, But Still A Buy
data.description:
Investment Overview: Viatris' Price Volatility 2020 — Present Day Explained

I last covered Viatris Inc. (NASDAQ:VTRS) back in June, assigning the company's stock a “Hold” rating. As someone who bought a few shares shortly after Viatris — formed via a merger of Pfizer's (PFE) “Established Brands” division with the generics drugmaker Mylan — began trading in November 2020, I have closely followed the company's progress. I have shared multiple notes with Seeking Alpha readers, calling the company a “screaming buy” back in 2020.

There has been plenty of share price volatility during Viatris' short life as a listed company. Initial enthusiasm drove the share price >$22 per share, but the company's early performance and outlook disappointed the market. By April 2021, the stock had fallen in value to ~$13 per share.

The announcement in February 2022 that Viatris would sell its biosimilar assets to Indian Pharma Biocon in a $3.4bn led to another market sell-off, shares sinking to a value of ~$10. The biosimilars division was viewed as one of Viatris' best shots at revitalizing a business that earned the majority of its revenues selling patent expired drugs like Viagra, the statin Lipitor, and depression med Lyrica, hence the disappointment.

Analysts were quick to downgrade expectations and ratings, and by September 2022 shares had sunk in value to ~$8.5. The M&A deal for eye disease specialist Oyster Pharma, and guidance for full-year revenues in 2022 of $16.2 — $16.7bn created a brief bull market for Viatris stock, but despite a Q2 2023 earnings beat, and guidance for 2023 revenues of $15.5B — $16.0B; adjusted EBITDA of $5.0B — $5.4B, and free cash flow of $2.3B — $2.7B, a downgrade from S&P global ratings caused shares to slip once again, falling to <$9 per share in October 2023.

That date marked the nadir for Viatris — so far at least. In February this year, Viatris stock reached highs of >$13.5 per share, as various hedge funds disclosed holdings, perhaps attracted by a generous dividend, paying $0.12 per share, which rose >6% as shares traded lower.

2023 earnings, reported at the end of February, dropped the share price yet again. Viatris reported:

Total Revenues of $15.4 Billion, U.S. GAAP Net Earnings of $54.7 Million, Adjusted EBITDA of $5.1 Billion, U.S. GAAP Net Cash Provided by Operating Activities of $2.8 Billion and Free Cash Flow of $2.4 Billion

For 2024, the company guided for revenues of $15.2bn — $15.75bn of revenues, and $2.70–$2.85 in adjusted earnings per share (“EPS”), which disappointed Wall Street, despite management declaring a $1bn addition to its share repurchase fund.

Viatris stock nearly fell <$10 in June, but has mounted a slight recovery since. Q2 earnings, reported in August, showed total revenues for the half year of $7.44bn, down 2% year-on-year, adjusted gross profit of $4.4bn, compared to $4.6bn in the prior year period, and a net loss of $(213m), compared to a net profit of $489m in 1H23. Free cash flow fell 36% year-on-year, to $885m.

For 2024, guidance was adjusted to $14.98bn — $15.48bn of revenues, adjusted EBITDA of $4.7bn — $5.01bn, free cash flow to $2.26bn — $2.66bn, and adjusted EPS of $2.66 — $2.81 - a forward price to earnings (“PE”) ratio of ~4.3x, and forward price to sales (“P/S”) ratio of ~1.1x.

Analysis — Viatris Valuation Conundrum

As I have discussed in several notes before today, the investment opportunity in relation to Viatris looks extremely attractive at first glance. It has a P/S ratio of 1.1x and P/E ratio of ~4.3x, low enough to suggest Viatris stock is a “screaming buy.” For example, among the top 250 or so of the highest valuation stocks listed on the S&P 500 (SP500), only the banks JPMorgan (JPM), Wells Fargo (WFC), and Goldman Sachs (GS) have a lower P/E.

The problem is that, as we can see below, Viatris' revenues are falling, not rising, and so is its operating income:

As we can see above, in December 2021, Viatris earned $4.3bn in revenues, and $620m in operating income, and $4.2bn, and $863m respectively in the following quarter.

In Q1 2024, revenues were $3.65bn, and operating income $441m, and in Q2, $3.8bn and $361m respectively. In essence, then, Viatris' P/E and P/S ratios are low because the market expects to revenues and income to fall, not rise.

This is mainly because sales of Viagra, Lyrica, Lipitor, and Pfizer's other Established Brands are only going to fall now that the products have no patent protection. They have generally been supplanted by newer or cheaper products in their commercial markets.

This helps to explain, for example, the market's disappointment at the Biocon deal — considered a key long-term driver of new revenues, its removal from the business lessened the prospect of the company reversing the trend of falling revenues.

It should also be noted that Viatris began life as a company saddled with considerable debt. At the end of 2020, long-term debt was reported as $22.43bn. Falling revenues surely undermine the company's ability to pay off debt, but with so much debt to repay, there is little funding available for R&D, or bolt-on M&A deals that could raise revenues.

A Long-Term Project — Is Phase 1 Complete — Can Phase 2 Growth Succeed

The current CEO Scott Smith was appointed in 2023, replacing Michael Goettler, and Smith has been charged with leading “Phase 2” of Viatris' business transformation. Here is how Smith described Viatris' progress and future at the beginning of the company's Q2 earnings call with analysts:

Among the many accomplishments we have, integrated two global companies, simplified and streamlined the business by completing our divestitures, returned the base business to growth, strengthened our balance sheet through significant debt paydown, returned capital to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases and importantly, we've already started to build our portfolio of innovative assets.

As we move forward in the second half of the year and beyond, we want to accelerate growth and shareholder return by building on the strength of our base business with an expanding portfolio of innovative, best-in-class patent-protected assets that have the potential for meaningful revenue growth and patient impact.

It is certainly true that Viatris has paid off a substantial portion of its long-term debt — which stood at $14.73bn as of the end of Q2 — and achieved only its second quarterly uplift in revenues in Q2. New product revenues in Q2 were $210m, management reported.

Nevertheless, that is less than 6% of total revenues, and in Q2, both generics revenues of $1.42bn and Established Brands revenues of $2.63bn were down 3% year-on-year. It seems clear that Viatris needs to add more product revenues, and at a faster rate than it is currently doing, if Phase 2 of the company's life is to be a success.

Encouragingly, new product revenues are expected by management to be $500 — $600m in FY24 — the advantage Viatris has over other companies is its established global network of manufacturing, distribution, and marketing infrastructure, inherited from Pfizer / Mylan. The company earned ~$1.3bn in Europe in Q2, ~$1bn in the US, ~$580m in emerging markets, $350m in Japan, Australia, and New Zealand (“JANZ”), and $539m in China.

Viatris is bullish on its new products' ability to offset falling revenues in generics. Established brands — selatogrel and cenerimod, indicated for acute myocardial infarction (“AMI”) and systemic lupus erythematosus respectively, and a result of Viatris collaboration with Swiss drug developer Idorsia — are both considered to have “blockbuster” (>$1bn revenues per annum) potential by management.

Within complex injectables, Wixela and Breyna — both indicated for asthma — are new product launches, while Chief R&D officer Philippe Martin advised on the Q2 earnings call:

Yes, regarding the complex generic, I think, we -- as you can see on this presentation that we've provided, we have over 250 products in the pipeline that are either under development or under regulatory review. So we think we'll be -- we know we'll have a steady flow of complex generic coming in every year, this year, and 2025 and so on. So we feel confident about our complex generic pipeline.

The list of targets for complex injectables even includes Ozempic and Wegovy, the weight loss / diabetes drugs developed by Novo Nordisk (NVO) and would likely command billions in revenues if Viatris can find its way through the Big Pharma's patent thickets.

MS therapy Glatiramer, opioid sparing treatment meloxicam, and a botox generic are exciting “novel and complex” generics in development, and recently launched Tyrvaya in eye disease could command peak revenues ~$400m, analysts have speculated. Across its entire eye care portfolio, Viatris expects to find another >$1bn in long-term annual revenues.

My Conclusion: No Longer Reliant On Viagra For Growth!

Please excuse the pun, but the point I am inelegantly making is that Viatris looks likely to come out on the right side of the growth conundrum, in my opinion. The company seems likely to be able to offset falling sales of Established Brands and older generics with new product launches. Some of these offer long-term patent protection, while others are generic versions of drugs driving double-digit billion revenues today, i.e., Wegovy, Ozempic.

As mentioned, the global infrastructure, while expensive to maintain, hands Viatris a competitive advantage so long as its pipeline does deliver. There are multiple signs that it is indeed delivering, and will continue — the recent R&D day presentation highlights multiple “blockbuster” opportunities. Besides, Viatris has spent years trimming its unnecessary infrastructure “fat.” producing a slicker, more cost-effective business framework for the future.

Debt is being paid off while the dividend remains generous, and buybacks are being completed. Viatris is highly likely to remain profitable for the foreseeable future, thanks to the ballast that Established Brands bring — management is promising at least $2.3bn of cash flow per annum for the foreseeable future.

If I had to issue a caveat around the investment case, I would say there is not a lot of detailed guidance for 2025 or longer-term — no pledges around revenue growth, or margins, apart from the promise of the $2.3bn cash flow. That is, however, understandable is some ways, as Viatris is a company in transition, operating globally, selling hundreds of products and working on hundreds more.

Viatris also says in its Q2 earnings press release that:

In July, the Company brought to substantial completion all divestitures with the closing of its Over-the-Counter (OTC) business to Cooper Consumer Health, a leading European over-the-counter drug manufacturer and distributor.

Bearing all of these things in mind, my conclusion is that, while it has been a slow and sometimes dispiriting journey since 2020, Viatris has essentially done what it set out to do. That is to ease the debt burden, inspire growth, and keep shareholders happy with dividends and buybacks. However, the risk remains that the company continues to shrink, and therefore becomes less valuable, but this risk is becoming more and more substantially diminished, based on my research.

The next target I hope to see the business achieve is a $20bn market cap valuation (current valuation is ~$14bn), which I believe may be achievable by 2026 / 2027 — amid encouraging contributions from new products, and revenues >$15bn per annum (revenues may still shrink YOY over the coming years, but the key factor is the performance of newer products, and profitability).

I am therefore looking forward to Viatris' Q3 earnings due 7th November, and more so to the longer-term performance of the company and the new product launches to come.

Meanwhile, Pfizer can't seem to stand up for falling down, despite its massive COVID-19 vaccine revenues windfall in 2021 and 2022. The company might even regret discarding its established Brands. Despite realizing a tax benefit and unloading lots of debt on Viatris, these products have held up remarkably well in their markets. This should allow the spinout co. to develop into a business that is beginning to look in better shape than its parent.
